1.text: The module docstring doubles as the package description. When users run help(ttok4bedrock), this is the first thing they see — it immediately communicates the library's purpose and compatibility promise.

6.text: These imports are at the top level even though they're only used indirectly. This is intentional — it ensures that import errors (missing boto3, wrong botocore version) surface immediately at import ttok4bedrock time, not deep inside a function call where the stack trace would be confusing.

7.text: boto3 is imported at module level to fail fast — if the AWS SDK isn't installed, the error appears at import time with a clear ModuleNotFoundError, not buried in a function call.

8.text: ClientError is imported but not used directly in this module. It's here so that users who do from ttok4bedrock import * don't get surprised by unhandled AWS exceptions — they can catch ClientError if needed.

9.text: The json module is imported for completeness — it's used by the core engine for message formatting, and having it available at the package level is a convenience.

11.text: Versioning follows semantic versioning. Having __version__ as a module attribute lets tools like pip show and programmatic checks access the version without parsing pyproject.toml.

12.text: __all__ explicitly limits the public API to just two functions. This is the facade pattern — users get a simple interface while the complex BedrockTokenCounter class stays internal. Anyone doing from ttok4bedrock import * gets exactly what they need.

15.text: The function signature mirrors ttok's API. The default model is set here at the API boundary, not inside BedrockTokenCounter — this keeps the core engine model-agnostic while giving SDK users a sensible default.

16.text: The text parameter is a plain string — no special formatting needed. The core engine handles wrapping it in the Bedrock message format internally.

17.text: The default model is the latest Claude Sonnet. Users can count tokens without knowing any Bedrock model IDs — the library picks a reasonable default.

18.text: Optional[str] means the region can be None, in which case boto3 uses its default credential chain to determine the region. This keeps the API simple for users who have AWS_DEFAULT_REGION set.

19.text: The return type int is explicit. Token counts are always whole numbers — there's no concept of a fractional token.

45.text: Lazy import — BedrockTokenCounter is imported inside the function body, not at module level. This means the heavy class (with its boto3 dependency chain) only loads when someone actually calls count_tokens(), keeping import ttok4bedrock fast.

46.text: A new counter is created on every call. This is a deliberate simplicity-over-performance trade-off — the SDK functions are stateless, which makes them safe to call from anywhere without worrying about shared state. The downside is that the LRU cache is lost between calls.

47.text: The SDK delegates immediately to the core engine. This one-liner is the entire implementation — the function exists purely to provide a convenient import path and default arguments.

56.text: The truncate() function mirrors the original ttok API. Having both count_tokens and truncate as top-level functions means migration from ttok is a one-line import change.

57.text: The text parameter is the input to truncate. The function returns the longest prefix that fits within max_tokens.

58.text: max_tokens is the budget. The adaptive truncation algorithm in the core engine finds the exact character boundary where the token count crosses this limit.

59.text: Same default model as count_tokens(). Consistency between the two functions means users can switch between counting and truncating without changing any parameters.

60.text: Same optional region parameter. The SDK functions are designed to be interchangeable — same signature pattern, same defaults, same behavior.

61.text: Returns a str — the truncated text. Unlike the core engine's truncate() which can optionally return metadata, the SDK always returns just the text for simplicity.

82.text: Same lazy-import-and-delegate pattern as count_tokens(). The symmetry is intentional — both SDK functions have identical structure, making the codebase predictable.

83.text: Same pattern as count_tokens() — new counter per call. For users who need cache persistence across multiple operations, the BedrockTokenCounter class is available directly.

84.text: Truncation delegates to the core engine's truncate() method, which implements the adaptive algorithm. The SDK doesn't expose return_metadata — that's an advanced feature only available through direct BedrockTokenCounter usage.
